content.text: Agent worker pods sit idle when the queue is empty and fall behind when jobs pile up. CPU-based autoscaling cannot fix this — workers spend most of their time blocked on Claude's HTTPS response, so CPU stays low even under heavy load. The right signal is
: the number of pending jobs directly tells Kubernetes how many workers to run. When depth is zero, scale to the minimum; when it grows, add pods proportionally.

attrs.alt: HPA scaling pipeline: Redis queue → queue exporter → Prometheus → HPA controller → worker deployment

content.text: How Kubernetes HPA Uses Custom Metrics
The Kubernetes HPA v2 API supports
metric types in addition to CPU/memory. The HPA computes the desired replica count using the formula:

desiredReplicas = ceil(currentMetricValue / targetMetricValuePerReplica)

# Example: queue depth = 47, target = 10 jobs per pod
# desiredReplicas = ceil(47 / 10) = 5
is the total queue depth and
is the number of jobs each worker can process concurrently (usually 1 for long Claude calls, higher for fast tool-only tasks).
Step 1: Expose Queue Depth as a Prometheus Metric
Write a lightweight exporter that reads queue depth from Redis and exposes it on a /metrics endpoint. Run it as a sidecar or as a standalone Deployment.

from prometheus_client import start_http_server, Gauge
import redis, time, os

r = redis.Redis(
    host=os.getenv("REDIS_HOST", "localhost"),
    port=int(os.getenv("REDIS_PORT", 6379)),
    decode_responses=True,
)

QUEUE_DEPTH = Gauge(
    "agent_queue_depth",
    "Number of pending jobs in each priority queue",
    ["priority"],
)

PRIORITY_KEYS = {
    "critical": "pq:critical",
    "high":     "pq:high",
    "normal":   "pq:normal",
    "batch":    "pq:batch",
}

def collect_metrics():
    for priority, key in PRIORITY_KEYS.items():
        depth = r.zcard(key)
        QUEUE_DEPTH.labels(priority=priority).set(depth)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    start_http_server(9090)
    print("Queue exporter running on :9090/metrics")
    while True:
        collect_metrics()
        time.sleep(10)

# Dockerfile for the exporter
FROM python:3.12-slim
RUN pip install prometheus-client redis
COPY queue_exporter.py /app/queue_exporter.py
CMD ["python", "/app/queue_exporter.py"]

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: queue-exporter
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: queue-exporter
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: queue-exporter
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port:   "9090"
        prometheus.io/path:   "/metrics"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: queue-exporter
          image: your-registry/queue-exporter:1.0.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 9090
          env:
            - name: REDIS_HOST
              value: redis.production.svc.cluster.local
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu:    "50m"
              memory: "32Mi"
            limits:
              cpu:    "100m"
              memory: "64Mi"
bridges Prometheus metrics to the Kubernetes custom metrics API so the HPA controller can read them. Install it with Helm:

helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm install prometheus-adapter prometheus-community/prometheus-adapter \
  --namespace monitoring \
  --set prometheus.url=http://prometheus.monitoring.svc.cluster.local \
  --set prometheus.port=9090
Configure the adapter to expose the queue depth metric per priority tier:

# prometheus-adapter ConfigMap (rules section)
rules:
  - seriesQuery: 'agent_queue_depth{namespace!="",pod!=""}'
    resources:
      overrides:
        namespace: {resource: "namespace"}
    name:
      matches: "agent_queue_depth"
      as:      "agent_queue_depth"
    metricsQuery: 'sum(agent_queue_depth{priority="normal"}) by (namespace)'
Create one HPA per worker tier. The
metric type queries Prometheus directly through the custom metrics API.

apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-normal-hpa
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: agent-worker-normal
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    - type: External
      external:
        metric:
          name: agent_queue_depth
          selector:
            matchLabels:
              priority: normal
        target:
          type:  AverageValue
          averageValue: "10"   # 1 pod per 10 pending jobs
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 30    # fast scale-up
      policies:
        - type:          Pods
          value:         4
          periodSeconds: 60
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 120   # slow scale-down to avoid thrash
      policies:
        - type:          Pods
          value:         2
          periodSeconds: 60
Use asymmetric stabilization windows: scale up fast (30s window) to prevent queue growth, scale down slowly (120s+ window) to avoid oscillation when jobs drain quickly.
Calibrating the Target Depth Per Replica
target determines how aggressively you scale. Derive it from your SLA and per-job processing time:

# Calibration formula
# target_depth_per_pod = SLA_seconds / avg_job_duration_seconds

# Example:
# SLA: 60 seconds queue wait
# Average Claude call: 8 seconds
# target = 60 / 8 = 7.5 → use 8 (round up for margin)

# At queue depth 40 with target 8:
# desiredReplicas = ceil(40 / 8) = 5
# All 40 jobs finish in parallel in ~8s → well within 60s SLA

# If avg job takes 20s and SLA is 60s:
# target = 60 / 20 = 3 → 1 pod per 3 jobs
Re-calibrate when the Claude model changes or when tool complexity shifts average job duration significantly. Monitor p95 queue wait time continuously to detect drift.
KEDA (Kubernetes Event-driven Autoscaling) is purpose-built for queue-depth scaling and supports Redis, SQS, RabbitMQ, and more natively — no custom exporter or Prometheus adapter needed.

# KEDA ScaledObject for Redis sorted set queue
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-scaledobject
  namespace: production
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: agent-worker-normal
  minReplicaCount: 2
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  cooldownPeriod:  120   # seconds before scale-down
  triggers:
    - type: redis
      metadata:
        address: redis.production.svc.cluster.local:6379
        listName: pq:normal        # Redis sorted set key
        listLength: "10"           # target jobs per replica
        activationListLength: "1"  # scale from 0 when any job appears
), saving cost on batch workloads that only run occasionally. The first job arriving wakes a pod via the

# Prometheus alert rules for queue-based autoscaling
groups:
  - name: agent-queue-alerts
    rules:
      - alert: QueueDepthHigh
        expr: agent_queue_depth{priority="critical"} > 50
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Critical queue depth > 50 for 2 minutes"

      - alert: QueueWaitTimeSLABreach
        expr: |
          agent_queue_oldest_job_age_seconds{priority="normal"} > 120
        for: 1m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Normal queue oldest job waiting > 120s (SLA breach)"

      - alert: HPAAtMaxReplicas
        expr: |
          kube_horizontalpodautoscaler_status_current_replicas
            == kube_horizontalpodautoscaler_spec_max_replicas
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "HPA at max replicas — consider raising maxReplicas"
Comparison: HPA with Custom Metrics vs KEDA

Queue-depth-based autoscaling solves the core problem of Claude worker scaling: CPU stays low regardless of load because workers are blocked on I/O. Expose queue depth as a Prometheus metric via a lightweight exporter, configure the Kubernetes HPA with an External metric pointing at that gauge, and set the target depth per pod based on SLA ÷ average job duration. For simpler setups or when you need scale-to-zero, KEDA with a Redis trigger achieves the same result with less configuration. In both cases, use asymmetric stabilization windows — fast scale-up, slow scale-down — to keep queue wait times below SLA without oscillating on short traffic dips.
